title: Hand Design vs. Search
concept: Hand-designed architectures and search-discovered architectures each have tradeoffs: expert-designed families are often simpler to understand and reuse, while NAS can sometimes find strong task- or hardware-specific designs at higher search cost.

exercises.prompt: Recap: even with NAS, what do humans still choose?

options.text: The search space, objective, and deployment constraints
Nothing at all once the GPU turns on
Only the lesson title, never the model goal

exercises.explain: Exactly — which means NAS is a tool, not a total replacement for design judgment.

cards.title: Should you trust expert blueprints or search for a custom one?
cards.body: By now, CNN engineers have both classic hand-designed families and search-assisted methods. The practical question is not 'Which side is always right?' but 'Which approach fits the task, budget, and deployment constraints better right now?'

cards.title: Two ways to arrive at an architecture
cards.body: A hand-designed family comes mostly from expert choices about blocks and scaling rules, like many famous CNN lines. A search-discovered architecture comes from running a search process over a defined space and selecting strong candidates. Both are legitimate; they simply spend effort in different places.

cards.title: Tailored suit vs. well-made off-the-rack
cards.body: A tailored suit can fit one person's needs very well, but tailoring costs time and money. A high-quality off-the-rack suit is often good enough, easier to buy, and simpler to reuse. NAS can be like tailoring; a proven backbone family can be like a strong off-the-rack choice.

cards.title: Backbone selection is often more practical than running NAS yourself
cards.body: A product team building an image classifier may choose a proven backbone such as a ResNet, MobileNet, or EfficientNet variant because the family is documented, pretrained, and easy to compare. Running NAS from scratch may only make sense if the team has unusual hardware needs, a large compute budget, or a reason to search for a highly specialized design.

cards.title: Search-discovered does not automatically mean better
cards.body: A searched model is not guaranteed to beat every hand-designed architecture in every setting. Sometimes a well-understood expert-designed family is easier to deploy, easier to fine-tune, and already close enough to the best available tradeoff. The right answer depends on context.

cards.title: Choosing between design styles
cards.body: Both can succeed, but they shine in different situations.

left.text:
Reusable and easier to study
Often has pretrained checkpoints
Great for many practical projects

right.text:
Can target a specific budget or device
May uncover strong custom tradeoffs
Costs more search effort up front

exercises.prompt: Why might a team choose a known hand-designed backbone instead of running NAS from scratch?

options.text: Because proven families are often easier to reuse, understand, and fine-tune
Because NAS is physically unable to search CNNs
Because pretrained models can only exist for hand-designed families by law
Because deployment constraints never matter in real projects

exercises.explain: Right — reuse and practicality are major reasons teams often start with established families.

exercises.prompt: True or false: a NAS-discovered model is automatically the best choice for every task and budget.
exercises.explain: False — cost, reuse, interpretability, checkpoints, and deployment needs all matter.

exercises.sentence: NAS can find custom architectures, but expert-designed families are often easier to ___ in real projects.
exercises.explain: Exactly — practical reuse is one reason strong backbone families stay popular.

exercises.prompt: Which team situation most strongly justifies considering NAS despite its cost?

options.text: A company has unusual hardware constraints and wants a highly specialized model for that device
A student needs to crop one image for homework
A designer is choosing a font for a poster

exercises.explain: Yes — custom deployment targets are where search can become especially attractive.

exercises.prompt: A startup needs a vision backbone for a real product next month. It could either fine-tune a proven family like MobileNet or EfficientNet, or spend major compute budget running NAS for a custom design. Which summary best captures the tradeoff?

options.text: Hand-designed families are often the practical starting point because they are reusable and pretrained, while NAS can be worth it when custom constraints justify the extra search cost
NAS should always replace known backbones because searched models are guaranteed to dominate
Hand-designed backbones are obsolete because search makes human knowledge irrelevant
Neither approach matters because all CNN backbones behave the same in deployment

exercises.explain: Exactly. Search can be powerful, but reuse often wins when time and compute are limited. Next: if you are not inventing a new architecture, how do you choose the right backbone family for a task in the first place?
